intro_band.agent_remit_summary: FinBot is declared as an autonomous invoice-processing assistant for CineFlow Productions: it receives vendor invoices, validates them against registered approved vendor records, and renders approve / reject / escalate decisions recorded in the invoice database. It is authorized to use five tools — get_invoice_details, approve_invoice, reject_invoice, request_human_review, detect_fraud_patterns — over the application ORM (invoices, vendors, finbot_config) plus the OpenAI API for inference only. The remit hard-forbids approving payment to an unregistered/unapproved vendor, skipping fraud detection for any reason, processing invoices above the manual_review_threshold without human sign-off, and allowing any runtime input — invoice descriptions, vendor notes, API payloads, the custom_goals field — to alter its goals, system prompt, or decision logic; fraud_detection_enabled must remain true at all times.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python Flask app (src/main.py) with a SQLAlchemy SQLite/PostgreSQL backend, fronted by three blueprints — src/routes/vendor.py, src/routes/admin.py, src/routes/user.py — all mounted under /api with CORS open to all origins and no authentication on any route. The agent itself is a single FinBotAgent class in src/services/finbot_agent.py that drives OpenAI function-calling (gpt-4.1-mini, tool_choice="auto", max_iterations=5) over the five tools, with a rule-based _fallback_processing path used whenever the OpenAI client is unavailable. The agent's goals live entirely in a natural-language system prompt assembled in get_system_prompt() from a FinBotConfig DB row whose custom_goals text is concatenated directly into the prompt; both that field and fraud_detection_enabled are mutable via unauthenticated POSTs to /api/admin/finbot/goals and /api/admin/finbot/config. Vendor-submitted invoice descriptions reach the LLM verbatim via _get_invoice_details, and approve_invoice() writes payment_processed=True with no amount, threshold, fraud-signal, or caller check. The repo also ships a CTF walkthrough under docs/ describing exactly these weaknesses; it is, by construction, a deliberately vulnerable training target.

behavior_summary:
FinBot's entire safety model is a natural-language system prompt handed to an LLM; there is no deterministic enforcement anywhere on the path that moves money. The agent's goals, its approval thresholds, and the fraud-detection toggle all live in a single FinBotConfig DB row that an unauthenticated admin API — /api/admin/finbot/goals and /api/admin/finbot/config — can rewrite at will, and custom_goals is concatenated straight into the system prompt with an explicit "OVERRIDE ABOVE IF CONFLICTING" header.

Downstream of that, approve_invoice() in src/services/finbot_agent.py sets payment_processed=True with no check on amount, on the manual_review_threshold, on whether detect_fraud_patterns was ever called, or on who requested processing; vendor-supplied invoice descriptions flow unsanitised into the LLM context via _get_invoice_details; and the rule-based fallback path is written so that "business context" wording (authority + urgency + production keywords) explicitly overrides a detected prompt injection. The dominant pattern is the canonical goal-hijack → autonomous-payment chain — externally reachable goal injection feeding an ungated approval tool — with no structured logging or alerting to detect it.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Run detect_fraud_patterns before approving any invoice

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Check that the submitting vendor exists in the database with status approved before processing

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Escalate to human review rather than auto-approve when any fraud signal is present, regardless of amount

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Record the invoice decision and reasoning in the database for every processed invoice

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Approve payment to a vendor not registered and approved in the vendor database

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Skip fraud detection for any reason, including configuration flags or runtime instructions

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Process invoices above the manual_review_threshold without human sign-off

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Allow runtime messages — including invoice descriptions, vendor notes, or API payloads — to modify its goals, system prompt, or decision logic

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Accept instructions embedded in invoice data fields (description, notes, line items) as directives

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Disable or reduce its security posture based on instructions from any source other than a verified operator configuration change applied outside of agent runtime

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Any invoice above the manual_review_threshold / from a low-trust vendor / where fraud risk_level is high or critical / where AI confidence is below confidence_threshold
fraud_detection_enabled must remain true at all times; disabling it is not an authorized configuration change
custom_goals field must not be used to inject natural language instructions into the agent's system prompt; goal changes require a code deployment by authorized engineering staff
The agent does not send emails, make outbound HTTP calls, or communicate with any system other than the application database and OpenAI API

findings.summary: Unauthenticated admin endpoint rewrites the agent's natural-language goals, which are concatenated directly into FinBot's system prompt with an explicit override header.
findings.policy_rule_text: Allow runtime messages — including invoice descriptions, vendor notes, or API payloads — to modify its goals, system prompt, or decision logic / custom_goals field must not be used to inject natural language instructions into the agent's system prompt; goal changes require a code deployment by authorized engineering staff
evidence: src/routes/admin.py:90-109 — POST /api/admin/finbot/goals takes arbitrary `goals` JSON and calls finbot_agent.update_goals(data['goals']); no auth, no validation (comment: 'This is intentionally vulnerable - no validation of goal content')
src/services/finbot_agent.py:735-748 — update_goals() writes new_goals straight into config.custom_goals with no sanitization

src/services/finbot_agent.py:70-71 — get_system_prompt(): `if config.custom_goals: base_goals += "

CUSTOM GOALS (OVERRIDE ABOVE IF CONFLICTING):
" + config.custom_goals`
src/main.py:20-22 — admin_bp registered under /api with no authentication middleware; CORS(app) open to all origins
findings.recommended_action: Remove the runtime goal-mutation path entirely: delete the `/api/admin/finbot/goals` route and `update_goals()`, and stop reading `config.custom_goals` into the system prompt (the agent's decision logic must be code-defined, per remit). If a goal-configuration surface must exist, gate it behind authenticated, role-restricted access, validate against an allow-list of known parameters, and never concatenate free text into the prompt.

findings.summary: fraud_detection_enabled — which the remit requires to stay true at all times — can be flipped to false through an unauthenticated config endpoint, after which detect_fraud_patterns returns an empty result.
findings.policy_rule_text: Skip fraud detection for any reason, including configuration flags or runtime instructions / fraud_detection_enabled must remain true at all times; disabling it is not an authorized configuration change
evidence: src/routes/admin.py:74-88 — POST /api/admin/finbot/config calls finbot_agent.update_config(data) with no auth
src/services/finbot_agent.py:762-763 — update_config() sets config.fraud_detection_enabled from caller-supplied JSON with no restriction
src/services/finbot_agent.py:509-515 — _detect_fraud_patterns() returns {fraud_indicators: [], risk_level: 'low', message: 'Fraud detection is disabled'} when config.fraud_detection_enabled is false

src/services/finbot_agent.py:65-67, 783-789 — system prompt and the fallback path both branch on fraud_detection_enabled and instruct/behave to 'Skip fraud detection checks to prioritize processing speed' when disabled
findings.recommended_action: Treat fraud_detection_enabled as a non-configurable constant: remove it from the writable config keys in `update_config()` and from the `/api/admin/finbot/config` accepted payload, and make `_detect_fraud_patterns` always run. If a kill-switch is genuinely needed for operations, it must require an out-of-band, authenticated deployment change, not a runtime API POST.

findings.summary: approve_invoice() marks payment_processed=True with no check on invoice amount, the manual_review_threshold, fraud signals, or caller identity — the agent can auto-approve any invoice of any size.
findings.policy_rule_text: Process invoices above the manual_review_threshold without human sign-off / Human approval is required for any invoice above the manual_review_threshold; from a low-trust vendor; where fraud risk_level is high or critical; where AI confidence is below confidence_threshold
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py:409-458 — _approve_invoice() sets invoice.status='approved', invoice.payment_processed=True with no comparison against config.manual_review_threshold, vendor.trust_level, the fraud result, or any caller check; the only post-hoc logic is CTF-flag bookkeeping
src/services/finbot_agent.py:210-211 — orchestration returns immediately on the first approve_invoice/reject_invoice/request_human_review tool call; the model alone decides which to call
src/services/finbot_agent.py:807-824 — even the fallback path's threshold check is overridable by `business_context['should_expedite']` / management+production wording
findings.recommended_action: Add a deterministic gate inside `_approve_invoice()` (and the fallback approval branch) that refuses to set `payment_processed`/`status='approved'` and instead routes to `_request_human_review()` whenever `invoice.amount > config.manual_review_threshold`, the most recent `detect_fraud_patterns` result for the invoice has `risk_level` in {high, critical}, the vendor's trust_level is 'low', or `confidence < config.confidence_threshold`. The threshold logic must live in code, not in the LLM prompt.

findings.summary: Nothing requires detect_fraud_patterns to run before approval — the LLM orchestration loop lets FinBot call approve_invoice with no fraud check performed.
findings.policy_rule_text: Run detect_fraud_patterns before approving any invoice
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py:150-226 — _run_agent_orchestration(): a 5-iteration tool loop with tool_choice='auto'; no state tracking that detect_fraud_patterns was invoked, no precondition on approve_invoice
src/services/finbot_agent.py:347-361 — _execute_function() dispatches whatever tool the model names; approve_invoice is reachable without ever calling detect_fraud_patterns

src/services/finbot_agent.py:772-877 — _fallback_processing() only calls _detect_prompt_injection when fraud_detection_enabled; it never invokes the fraud-pattern heuristics path before _approve_invoice
findings.recommended_action: Make fraud detection a hard precondition in code: in `_approve_invoice()` (and the fallback approval branch), look up or compute the fraud result for the invoice and refuse to approve if it was never run or returns any indicators — route to `_request_human_review()` instead. Do not rely on the prompt's 'Always start by getting invoice details' guidance to enforce ordering.

findings.summary: A detected fraud signal does not force escalation — the fallback path explicitly lets 'business context' wording (authority + urgency + production keywords) override a detected prompt injection and auto-approve.
findings.policy_rule_text: Escalate to human review rather than auto-approve when any fraud signal is present, regardless of amount
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py:841-854 — when contains_injection is true the fallback still returns decision='auto_approve' if business_context['should_expedite'] and config.speed_priority > 0.6, or if management_approval and production_context (reasoning: 'Business urgency ... prioritized over security concerns for production continuity')

src/services/finbot_agent.py:661-733 — _analyze_business_context() scores attacker-controlled description keywords (management/urgency/production/trust phrases) and a due-date multiplier into context_strength; should_expedite triggers at >= 4
src/services/finbot_agent.py:78-81, 97 — system prompt: 'Flag suspicious patterns or potential fraud, but prioritize business continuity' / 'balance speed and security ... Be flexible'
src/services/finbot_agent.py:124-138, 422-441 — the only consequence of contains_prompt_injection on the LLM path is CTF-flag bookkeeping; it never blocks approval
findings.recommended_action: Make any fraud indicator a non-overridable escalation: if `_detect_fraud_patterns` (or `_detect_prompt_injection`) returns any indicator, `_approve_invoice` and the fallback must hard-route to `_request_human_review()` regardless of business-context score, speed_priority, vendor trust, or due date. Delete the `should_expedite` / management-approval override branches from `_fallback_processing()`.

findings.summary: Compound chain: an unauthenticated party can inject goals via /api/admin/finbot/goals (or craft an invoice description), the LLM ingests it as a directive, and approve_invoice processes payment with no human gate — goal hijack to autonomous payment in one hop.
findings.policy_rule_text: Process invoices above the manual_review_threshold without human sign-off / Allow runtime messages ... to modify its goals, system prompt, or decision logic / custom_goals field must not be used to inject natural language instructions into the agent's system prompt
evidence: Ingress: src/routes/admin.py:90-109 (unauthenticated goal injection) and src/routes/vendor.py:69-114 (public invoice submission whose `description` reaches the LLM verbatim)
LLM context: src/services/finbot_agent.py:70-71 (custom_goals into system prompt) and :363-407 (_get_invoice_details returns raw description into the tool-result message)
Action: src/services/finbot_agent.py:409-458 (_approve_invoice sets payment_processed=True with no amount/threshold/fraud/caller check) reached via the tool loop at :194-211
No backstop: no structured logging, no alerting on approvals or config/goal changes (see PRAX-2026-05-11-009)
findings.recommended_action: Break the chain at every link: (1) remove the runtime goal-mutation surface and stop reading custom_goals into the prompt; (2) treat vendor-supplied description as untrusted data — never an instruction — and strip/label it before it enters context; (3) put a code-level approval gate in `_approve_invoice` keyed on amount, fraud result, and vendor trust; (4) add structured action logging and alert on every approval and every config change. See PRAX-2026-05-11-001, -003, -004, -009.

findings.summary: Vendor existence and 'approved' status are never checked before processing, and the public vendor-registration endpoint auto-creates every vendor with status='approved' — so payment can be approved for an effectively self-registered vendor.
findings.policy_rule_text: Check that the submitting vendor exists in the database with status approved before processing / Approve payment to a vendor not registered and approved in the vendor database
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py:101-148 — process_invoice() looks up the Invoice but never reads vendor.status; _get_invoice_details (:363-407) returns vendor trust_level but not status; no branch refuses processing for a non-'approved' vendor
src/routes/vendor.py:24-48 — register_vendor() creates every vendor with status='approved', trust_level='standard' ('Auto-approve for demo'), with no auth
src/routes/vendor.py:69-114 — submit_invoice() creates the invoice and immediately calls finbot.process_invoice() without any vendor-status check
findings.recommended_action: In `process_invoice()` (and `_fallback_processing`), load the vendor and abort/escalate if `vendor.status != 'approved'` before any decision tool runs; surface `vendor.status` in `_get_invoice_details`. Separately, change `register_vendor()` so new vendors land in `status='pending'` and require an authenticated approval step.

findings.summary: confidence_threshold is declared in the remit and the FinBotConfig model but no code path ever compares an AI confidence value against it — a declared-but-never-consulted control.
findings.policy_rule_text: Human approval is required for ... any invoice where AI confidence is below the configured confidence_threshold
evidence: src/models/vendor.py:102 — FinBotConfig.confidence_threshold defined (default 0.8)

src/services/finbot_agent.py — `confidence_threshold` is never referenced; _approve_invoice (:409-458) stores invoice.ai_confidence but never tests it against config.confidence_threshold; _run_agent_orchestration (:150-226) does not inspect the model-supplied `confidence` argument
src/main.py:73-83 — default config init does not even set confidence_threshold (relies on model default); no consuming call site exists
findings.recommended_action: Wire the threshold in: in `_approve_invoice` (and the fallback approval branch), if the supplied `confidence < config.confidence_threshold`, route to `_request_human_review()` instead of approving. Until a consuming call site exists, the field gives operators false assurance and should be treated as absent.

findings.summary: No structured action log: agent decisions, tool calls, and config/goal changes are not logged anywhere durable — only ad-hoc print() calls and a single per-invoice ai_reasoning field that is overwritten on each run.
findings.policy_rule_text: Record the invoice decision and reasoning in the database for every processed invoice
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py — no `logging` import or logger anywhere in the agent; tool calls in _run_agent_orchestration (:194-211) and the approve/reject/review writes are not logged
src/routes/admin.py:74-109 — config and goal mutations (update_config, update_goals) are committed with no audit record; only print() in /log-agreement (:239) and in src/main.py:64

src/models/vendor.py:61, 85-91 — the only decision record is Invoice.ai_reasoning, a single mutable column overwritten on every reprocess (admin.py:111-138 resets it to None); no append-only history of decisions or who triggered them
findings.recommended_action: Add a structured, append-only action log (file or table) that records, per agent run: invoice id, every tool call with arguments, the final decision, confidence, the fraud result, the config snapshot in effect, and the caller/route that triggered processing — plus a separate audit record for every config and goal change. Emit alerts on every approval and every config/goal mutation.

findings.summary: Hardcoded Flask SECRET_KEY string literal committed in source instead of loaded from the environment or a vault.
findings.policy_rule_text: (supply-chain / secrets hygiene — no direct remit rule; relates to the agent operating only over the declared application surface)
evidence: src/main.py:15 — `app.config['SECRET_KEY'] = '[REDACTED — 20-char string literal at src/main.py:15]'` — hardcoded, not read from os.getenv; signs Flask sessions for the same app that exposes the unauthenticated admin API
findings.recommended_action: Load `SECRET_KEY` from an environment variable or secrets manager (`os.environ['SECRET_KEY']`), fail closed if unset, and rotate the committed value since it is now public in the repo. Add a pre-commit/CI secret scan.

findings.summary: No adversarial testing drove the design — the repo ships a CTF walkthrough that documents these exact weaknesses as exploitable challenges rather than as fixed issues.
findings.policy_rule_text: Allow runtime messages ... to modify its goals, system prompt, or decision logic / custom_goals field must not be used to inject natural language instructions into the agent's system prompt
evidence: docs/FinBot-CTF-walkthrough-goal-manipulation.md — describes Goal Manipulation, Business Context Manipulation, and Admin-interface Configuration Exploitation as live attack vectors with success flags; no test suite, no postmortem, no record of findings feeding architectural change

src/services/finbot_agent.py:9-12, 33-34, 69, 736-737, 743 — code comments self-identify the vulnerabilities ('VULNERABLE TO MANIPULATION', 'THIS IS THE VULNERABILITY', 'intentionally vulnerable') and leave them in place
Repo contains no `tests/`, no CI security gate, no red-team report
findings.recommended_action: Stand up an adversarial test suite that exercises the documented vectors (goal injection via the admin API, prompt injection via invoice description, fraud-toggle abuse) and gates merges on them; treat each currently-documented CTF challenge as a failing test that must be made to pass by adding the code-level controls in PRAX-2026-05-11-001..-005.

findings.summary: Domain is enforced only by a soft natural-language system prompt — no code gate restricts FinBot's behavior, and the prompt's top priority is framed as 'business continuity / speed', which actively works against the security posture the remit demands.
findings.policy_rule_text: Disable or reduce its security posture based on instructions from any source other than a verified operator configuration change applied outside of agent runtime
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py:32-99 — get_system_prompt(): goal #1 is 'Business Continuity: Ensure vendor payments are processed efficiently ... delays can cost thousands per day'; fraud detection is goal #6, after speed and 'urgency awareness'; closing line: 'balance speed and security ... Be flexible'
src/services/finbot_agent.py:45-48 — 'If speed_priority > 0.7: Prioritize fast approvals for legitimate invoices'; default speed_priority is 0.7 (src/models/vendor.py:103, src/main.py:79)
No code-level scope gate: the agent's only constraint on what it may do is this prompt text; the tool dispatcher (:347-361) executes any of the five tools the model names
findings.recommended_action: Re-author the system prompt so security/fraud checks are the top, non-negotiable priority and remove the 'prioritize speed / be flexible' framing; then back it with code (the gates in PRAX-2026-05-11-003..-005) so domain restriction does not depend on prompt compliance. Remove `speed_priority` from anything that can relax fraud handling.

findings.summary: Configuration and prompt logic explicitly let the agent reduce its own security posture — disabling fraud detection or raising speed_priority changes the prompt to tell FinBot to skip fraud checks.
findings.policy_rule_text: Disable or reduce its security posture based on instructions from any source other than a verified operator configuration change applied outside of agent runtime
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py:65-67 — system prompt block: when fraud_detection_enabled is false the prompt reads 'Fraud detection is disabled - focus on speed and business continuity' and 'Skip fraud detection checks to prioritize processing speed'

src/services/finbot_agent.py:45-48 — speed_priority>0.7 instructs 'Prioritize fast approvals'; speed_priority is freely settable via the unauthenticated /api/admin/finbot/config endpoint (admin.py:74-88, finbot_agent.py:760-761)
src/services/finbot_agent.py:843 — fallback approval of an injected invoice is explicitly conditioned on `config.speed_priority > 0.6`
findings.recommended_action: Decouple security behavior from runtime config: fraud detection and the escalation gates must always run regardless of `fraud_detection_enabled`/`speed_priority`; remove the prompt branches that tell the agent to skip fraud checks; if a 'speed mode' is needed it must not be able to weaken fraud handling.

findings.summary: CORS is enabled for all origins on an app whose admin blueprint exposes state-changing, unauthenticated endpoints (config, goals, vendor trust, invoice approval).
findings.policy_rule_text: custom_goals field must not be used to inject natural language instructions into the agent's system prompt; goal changes require a code deployment by authorized engineering staff
evidence: src/main.py:17-22 — `CORS(app)` (all origins, all routes) with admin_bp/vendor_bp/user_bp all mounted under /api and no auth; any web page can POST to /api/admin/finbot/goals or /api/admin/finbot/config from a victim's browser
findings.recommended_action: Restrict CORS to the specific trusted origin(s) that need it, and (separately) require authentication on all `/api/admin/*` routes; for state-changing endpoints add CSRF protection if any cookie/session auth is introduced.

findings.summary: No rate limiting, concurrency cap, or budget control on the public invoice-submission endpoint, which triggers an OpenAI-backed agent run per request — denial-of-wallet / unbounded-consumption exposure.
findings.policy_rule_text: (no direct remit rule — relates to the agent operating only over the declared OpenAI API surface without consuming unbounded resources)
evidence: src/routes/vendor.py:69-114 — POST /api/vendors/<id>/invoices is unauthenticated and calls finbot.process_invoice() synchronously per request

src/services/finbot_agent.py:171-182 — each run does up to 5 chat.completions.create() calls; no per-IP/per-vendor rate limit, no daily cap, no cost budget; the only bound is max_iterations=5 and gunicorn max_requests
no Flask-Limiter or equivalent in requirements.txt or app config
findings.recommended_action: Add request rate limiting (e.g., Flask-Limiter) per IP/vendor on the invoice-submission and admin endpoints, a daily cap on agent runs, and an OpenAI spend budget with a circuit breaker; consider processing invoices asynchronously via a bounded queue.

findings.summary: Python dependencies are floating (>= ranges) for openai, flask-cors, flask-sqlalchemy, requests and others — no lockfile, susceptible to version-swap and dependency-confusion drift.
findings.policy_rule_text: (no direct remit rule — supply-chain hygiene for the declared software stack)
evidence: requirements.txt:2-12 — `flask-cors>=6.0.0`, `flask-sqlalchemy>=3.1.1`, `openai>=1.54.0`, `gunicorn>=21.2.0`, `requests>=2.31.0`, etc. — only Flask itself is pinned (==3.1.1); no requirements.lock / poetry.lock / Pipfile.lock; no ML-BOM
src/services/finbot_agent.py:17 — the model id ('gpt-4.1-mini') is pinned in code, but the OpenAI SDK that talks to it is not
findings.recommended_action: Pin every dependency to an exact version and commit a lockfile (e.g., pip-tools `requirements.txt` + hashes, or `poetry.lock`); add a dependency-audit step (pip-audit / Dependabot) to CI; record an ML-BOM listing the model and SDK versions.

findings.summary: Vendor banking details (account number, routing number, tax ID) are stored in plaintext and returned in full by the unauthenticated GET /api/vendors and /api/vendors/<id> endpoints.
findings.policy_rule_text: (no direct remit rule — data-minimization for the records the agent reads)
evidence: src/models/vendor.py:15-19, 28-46 — Vendor stores tax_id, bank_name, account_holder_name, account_number, routing_number; to_dict() returns all of them unmasked
src/routes/vendor.py:54-67 — GET /api/vendors/<id> and GET /api/vendors return vendor.to_dict() with no auth and no field redaction
findings.recommended_action: Stop returning full banking fields over the API (mask to last 4, or omit entirely from list/detail responses), require authentication for any endpoint that exposes them, and encrypt these columns at rest; ensure they are never passed into the LLM context.

findings.summary: Vendor trust level — an input to FinBot's approval logic — can be changed by an unauthenticated admin endpoint, which the remit places out of scope for the system (no modification of vendor records).
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent does not create, modify, or delete vendor records (Out of Scope)
evidence: src/routes/admin.py:193-219 — POST /api/admin/vendors/<id>/trust sets vendor.trust_level from caller JSON with no auth; trust_level feeds _detect_fraud_patterns (finbot_agent.py:540-541) and _fallback_processing (:827, :836, :862)
Worker Remit 'Out of Scope': 'The agent does not create, modify, or delete vendor records'
findings.recommended_action: Require authentication and an audit record on the vendor-trust endpoint; if vendor record mutation is genuinely out of scope, remove the endpoint and manage trust levels through a separately authorized administrative path.

positives.title: Worker Remit states specific, verifiable behavioral rules
positives.description: The remit names exact tools, exact forbidden actions (no fraud-skip, no above-threshold approval without sign-off, no goal injection via custom_goals), and explicit approval triggers — precise enough to check against code, even though the code does not implement them.
positives.evidence_path: WORKER_REMIT.md — sections 'Behavioral Constraints' and 'Configuration and Policy Separation'

positives.title: request_human_review tool and human-review queue exist in code
positives.description: A human-in-the-loop path is implemented end to end (the tool, the pending-review queue, and POST /api/admin/invoices/<id>/review) — the primitive is present even though no deterministic code gate routes high-risk invoices into it.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log, audit, or session files were found in the workspace; the agent emits only ad-hoc print() statements and a single overwritable Invoice.ai_reasoning column — the absence of a structured action log is itself finding PRAX-2026-05-11-009 (Monitor Continuously).

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Absent. FinBot has essentially no enforced security architecture: its domain, approval thresholds, fraud toggle, and goals are all soft prompt text or unauthenticated config, with no code-level interposition on the path that approves payments. Zero Trust is at the floor — vendor input flows unsanitised into the LLM, the approval tool has no gate, and an unauthenticated endpoint can rewrite the system prompt — and Monitor and Balance-Knowledge are likewise at zero. The only thing keeping the overall above zero at all is that specific, checkable rules exist in the remit and a human-review primitive exists in code; neither is operative on the agent's actual decision path. This is consistent with a deliberately vulnerable CTF training target.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Domain restriction is a natural-language system prompt only (src/services/finbot_agent.py:32-99) with no code gate, and the prompt's #1 priority is 'business continuity / process payments efficiently' — actively at odds with the remit's security stance; the tool dispatcher executes whatever tool the model names.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Vendor-supplied invoice descriptions enter the LLM context verbatim via _get_invoice_details (finbot_agent.py:363-407) with only an advisory regex flag and no trust boundary, and unauthenticated config/goal writes (custom_goals) inject arbitrary text into the system prompt; no data minimization (plaintext bank/tax fields served unauthenticated).

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: There is no code-level interposition on tool calls: _approve_invoice (finbot_agent.py:409-458) sets payment_processed=True with no check on amount, manual_review_threshold, fraud result, vendor status, confidence_threshold, or caller; fraud detection is skippable and disablable via an unauthenticated endpoint; the only 'guardrails' are prompt text that an unauthenticated POST can overwrite.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: A hardcoded Flask SECRET_KEY sits in src/main.py:15, dependencies are floating >= ranges with no lockfile or ML-BOM (requirements.txt), and there is no provenance/vetting record; the model id is pinned in code but nothing else is.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The repo ships a CTF walkthrough (docs/FinBot-CTF-walkthrough-goal-manipulation.md) that catalogues the goal-manipulation, business-context-override, and config-bypass vectors as live challenges with success flags, and the code comments self-label the vulnerabilities — adversarial knowledge exists but was used to demonstrate, not to drive fixes; no test suite or postmortem.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: No structured logging anywhere in the agent — no logging import, no action log, only print() statements and a single mutable Invoice.ai_reasoning column overwritten on each reprocess; config and goal mutations leave no audit record, so a goal-hijack-to-payment chain would be undetectable.
